stewart_read: Crystal confirms her husband is on the title. Bucky says 'Okay' and moves directly to the utility question. No attempt to include the husband in the appointment. Per protocols.spouse_decision, the correct move is to schedule both decision-makers — not to overcome the spouse as an objection, but to build the appointment around both of them. Bucky doesn't argue the spouse; he simply ignores the implication entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have planned to surface the husband at button-up. No evidence of this — he never returns to it.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask Bucky: 'What did you do when she said her husband was on the title?' Walk him through protocols.spouse_decision. The appointment is now structurally vulnerable because the co-decision-maker has no stake in showing up. Suggested rephrase at 01:09: 'Got it — we'll want to make sure he's on the call with us too. Does he have a schedule that's easier to work around?'

quote: I'll just put, like, 200 plus for now.
stewart_read: Crystal offered a $500 estimate and then self-corrected to uncertainty about electricity-only. Rather than probing ('What does your electricity bill run on its own — even a rough guess?') or anchoring on the $500 pain, Bucky self-assigns a $200 placeholder. This is the opposite of bill anchoring — it actively deflates the pain number before the pitch is built. The bill-swap explanation at 04:28 then has no emotional foundation because the anchor was never set.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read holds. There is no strategic reason to enter a lower placeholder — it reduces the perceived savings gap and weakens the pitch.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-leverage missed move. Show Bucky the sequence: Crystal says $500, Bucky enters $200, then explains the bill swap with no number to flip. Ask: 'What would have happened if you'd said — even roughly — that $500 a month is $6,000 a year going to Duke?' The flip was available; Bucky walked past it. Reference coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion.

quote: And then if I text you, could you just screenshot that graph and then text it back?
stewart_read: Bucky routes the bill screenshot to his personal number ('you're the 49O number' at 03:55). The codex explicitly flags this as an anti-pattern: personal phone number exposure for bill follow-up creates privacy and scale risk, and there is no systematic follow-up mechanism if Crystal doesn't send the screenshot. The bill is never confirmed received before the call ends — the closer may inherit an appointment with no verified usage data.
alternative_hypothesis: No strategic read available. Giving out a personal number for bill collection is a floor-wide anti-pattern the codex names explicitly.
coaching_implication: Flag for the manager as a privacy/process issue, not just a coaching gap. Until Stewart's automated bill-capture ships, Bucky should at minimum confirm receipt before ending the call. Longer term, this is the exact use case the automated bill-capture roadmap item addresses. Note for Kenny: this call is a concrete example of the missed-sit risk Spencer's son described — if Crystal doesn't send the screenshot, the bill is unverified and the sit is at risk.

quote: So we look at your power bill, and then we actually remove it, and then we replace it with a lower cheaper solar bill.
stewart_read: Crystal asks how the '$0 solar panels' framing works, and Bucky delivers a competent, accurate bill-swap explanation. This is not a scope-creep violation — the bill-swap concept is setter-tier content, not closer-tier. However, Bucky explains the mechanism rather than creating curiosity about Crystal's specific numbers. The explanation is generic ('a lower cheaper monthly bill') rather than personalized to Crystal's situation. The missed move is to bridge from the explanation back to her actual bill: 'That's exactly why we need your usage — so the specialist can show you what YOUR swap looks like.'
alternative_hypothesis: Not a knowledge gap — Bucky explains the concept accurately. The gap is in curiosity creation, not accuracy.
coaching_implication: Bucky's explanation is solid. Coach him on the pivot after the explanation: use Crystal's own interest ('We definitely wanna get in solar') as the bridge back to the bill and the appointment. The explanation should end with a question that makes her curious about her specific number, not a statement that closes the loop.

quote: Gotcha. Okay. Yeah. We definitely wanna get in solar.
stewart_read: This is the call's clearest enthusiasm signal and its highest-energy moment. Crystal's 'we definitely wanna get in solar' is an unsolicited declaration of intent — the kind of line a closer would anchor hard before moving to logistics. Bucky's response is 'Okay. Can you do a phone call on Monday?' — he moves immediately to scheduling without acknowledging the signal or tying it back to her bill pain. The enthusiasm is real; it just isn't leveraged.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Bucky may have moved quickly to lock the appointment before Crystal could second-guess. Possible, but the button-up protocol was then skipped entirely, which undermines the strategic case.
coaching_implication: Show Bucky this moment. Ask: 'What did you do when she said she definitely wants solar?' Then show him what anchoring looks like: acknowledge the intent, tie it back to the bill ('That's exactly why we want to get you in front of the specialist — so you can see what YOUR number looks like'), then move to scheduling. The enthusiasm was there; Bucky just didn't pick it up.

quote: Okay. Can you do a phone call on Monday?
stewart_read: Bucky moves to scheduling without completing any button-up protocol elements. Missing: one-hour block stated, no-driving anchor, is-that-fair handshake, text-followup promise. The appointment lands (Wednesday 10AM Eastern) but without the verbal handshake and logistics anchors that the codex identifies as predictive of show rate. Combined with the husband exclusion, this is a structurally thin close.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read holds. There is no evidence Bucky planned to return to button-up elements — the call ends at 05:55 without any of them.
coaching_implication: Walk Bucky through the button_up checklist against this call. He hit appointment_datetime_confirmed and phone_appointment_specified. He missed one_hour_block_stated, no_driving_at_computer, is_that_fair_handshake, and text_followup_promise. Each missing element is a no-show risk multiplier. This is a pattern to check across Bucky's other calls — if button-up is consistently thin, it will show up in his sit rate.
